item.title: Space Cadet Pinball on Linux - Stephen Brennan
item.description: Stephen Brennan's personal website and blog.

item.title: Louis Rossmann tells 3D printer maker Bambu Lab to ‘Go (Bleep) yourself’ over its threatened lawsuit against enthusiast — Right to Repair advocate offers to pay the legal fees for a threatened OrcaSlicer developer
item.description: Bambu Labs controversy heats up.
item.author: (https://www.tomshardware.com/author/denise-bertacchi)

item.title: GrapheneOS (@GrapheneOS@grapheneos.social)
item.description: Apple and Google are gradually expanding their use of hardware-based attestation. They're convincing a growing number of services to adopt it. Google's Play Integrity API and Apple's App Attest API are very similar. Apple brought it to the web via Privacy Pass, which Google intends on doing too.

item.title: HomeGrown Devlog: Programming the Research System
item.description:
Wishlist HomeGrown on Steam: https://store.steampowered.com/app/1894560/HomeGrown/
Play my previous game "Equilinox": https://store.steampowered.com/app/853550/Equilinox/
Support the channel on Patreon and get access to the game & code for Homegrown, the city-builder, and Equilinox: https://www.patreon.com/thinmatrix
Follow the game's progress on my social media: Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/thinmatrix/ Twitter: https://twitter.com/ThinMatrix Bluesky: https://bsky.app/profile/thinmatrix.bsky.social Trello: https://trello.com/b/W3zkIJTM/farm Email: thinmatrix@gmail.com
Background music by Jamal Green: https://open.spotify.com/artist/50jTMgIPZrjKFgHbCvoeRt?si=Z-xfUXEXQCuGdGRy7qf9Ug&dl_branch=1
#devlog #HomeGrown
item.author: (ThinMatrix)

item.title: France Moves to Break Encrypted Messaging
item.description: France's parliament just endorsed the one idea every cryptographer on earth has already debunked and they're calling it a compromise.

item.title: bits from the release team
The left-wing case for AI
item.description: In Many anti-AI arguments are conservative arguments I argued that left-wing anti-AI sentiment1 is partly a backlash to two unrelated events around the rise of ChatGPT: the crypto mania of 2022 and the pro-Donald-Trump push many big tech CEOs made in 2024. If the timing had been different, we could have had a real pro-AI faction on the left. What would that look like?

item.title: GitHub - imtomt/ymawky: MacOS Web Server written entirely in ARM64 assembly
item.description: MacOS Web Server written entirely in ARM64 assembly - imtomt/ymawky

item.title: You should read Programming as Theory Building
item.description: When I finished reading Peter Naur’s Programming as Theory Building my first thought was “How come nobody ever told me to read this?” I ended up reading it multiple times, as I attempted to collect my thoughts on why it makes so much sense.

item.title: Apple is increasing my cortisol levels
item.description: My blog, where I attempt to collect my thoughts and share the occasional interesting topic with others

item.title: I’ve banned query strings — Chris Morgan
item.description: 🗓️ 2026-05-08 • Tagged /web, /opinions, /meta=only

item.title: The Intolerable Hypocrisy of Cyberlibertarianism
item.description: I like the Internet. I am old enough to remember the pre-Internet era and despite the younger generations pining for those simpler days, I was there. Paper maps were absolutely horrible, just you and a compass in your car on the side of the road in the middle of the

item.title: GrapheneOS fixes Android VPN leak Google refused to patch
item.description: GrapheneOS has released a new update that fixes a recently disclosed Android VPN bypass flaw capable of leaking a user’s real IP address.

item.title: Please Make Mistakes
item.description: When your team adopts AI, mistakes are inevitable and necessary. Your job as code owner is to catch them before customers see them, and improve your instruction files so they don't happen twice.

item.title: Alex-Dahl
item.description: I have been a manager for quite some time. I have seen plenty of different behaviors play out. Eagerness. Apathy. Rage. I thought I could navigate all of them and make sure they could direct their feelings to align with the organization's goals. I saw myself as a skilled corporate diplomat. Until I met Elias.

item.title: it's time to talk about agentic "remote control"
item.description: tldr: skip the vendor remote-control features. roll your own with wireguard, tmux, and ssh. cheaper, more flexible, and you stop handing third parties a live shell into your dev box.

item.title: The Mirror Is Part of the Machine | Yusuf Aytas
item.description: Why telemetry explodes into cost and noise, and how to fix it: treat every signal as a decision, not exhaust, and govern ownership before it's too late.
item.author: (Yusuf Aytas)

item.title: Write programs you can still hack when you feel dumb
item.description: … so you can still understand and change them, when you can only work half an hour

item.title: How to brush your teeth at work
item.description: The homepage of Joshua Liebow-Feeser

item.title: I still like Jenkins
item.description: Before GitHub Actions, before GitLab CI/CD, and before the panoply of startups all promising to help you build your software faster and with less hassle, there was the humble, open source Jenkins. Or perhaps I should say is: Jenkins is still actively maintained to this day, and has actually been in use in basically every single place I have worked since graduating from high school, although not everyone who was using it was exactly happy with it.

item.title: The Morse Code Hack That Made an AI Agent Spend $200,000
item.description: Dave explains the Grok/Bankrbot exploit that caused an AI Agent to spend almost $200K in tokens!
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.title: Money is renewable. Time isn't.
item.description: We built a whole industry to count our dollars. We barely count our hours. The asymmetry is backwards.

item.title: Learning Beyond Gradients
item.description: Learning Beyond Gradients

item.title: This blog post tells the time

item.title: A tale of two artisans – Koas.dev
item.description: Let me tell you about what alcoholics refer to as a moment of clarity I had last night.

item.title: The Mismeasure of Open Source
item.description: The streetlight effect in project-health scoring

item.title: How we accidentally recreated old FB
item.description: Background We started off with a simple idea for a product; we wanted to re-use the chat interface for everything except chat. It was supposed to be just for photos but expandable later. The idea was that most people already share their photos with others on WhatsApp or some other messaging service, which technically also ’tags’ the people who were there. So, why not turn the act of sharing into the act of organizing as well?

item.title: Lies, damned lies, and Elastic's benchmarks
item.description: It's a spicy title, but one that is warranted. Elastic recently published a blog post titled 30x faster than Prometheus, that claimed that Elastic's new TSDS timeseries engine is much better than Prometheus. When I started reading it, I was curious; maybe there are useful optimizations we can learn about.

item.title: Yesterday I had some news that has left me feeling anxious, happy but also conflicted all at the same time.
item.description: My very first blog post was written the day before my last counselling session with, Angel,It is called The last day.My counselling sessions with her were a 100 percent positive experience and she …

item.description: Nate Anderson:

item.title: The Fast Way to Sweden - BGP Routing Experiments
item.description: Learning about selectively announcing prefixes with BGP to get traffic to its destination faster

item.title: Laptops all have built-in security tokens these days
item.description: I’ve been a fan of security tokens for a decade now and have accrued quite a collection. This redundancy isn’t a bad thing, as security tokens are easily misplaced and the only way to recover from a lost token is using a second token that is also registered with the service you’re trying to access. I use security tokens whenever I can! SSH authentication, universal two-factor (U2F) authentication, passwordless local login, sudo command elevation, and git commit signing are all things I use security tokens for every day. When I take my laptop traveling, there also travels a yubikey. However, it took me an oddly long time to realize that I’m a relic of a bygone era. Laptops and smartphones all have built-in security tokens these days! I’ve been carrying around yubikeys when an even better one is built right into my macbook. This post is about how I use security tokens, and how I configured my laptop’s secure element to replace my yubikey collection.
